figures.gloss: The enigmatic theurgist (c. 1727-1774) who built the Ordre des Chevaliers Macons Elus Coens de l'Univers on top of the Masonic craft degrees, teaching ceremonial 'operations' — equinoctial invocations seeking luminous 'passes' as proof of contact with reconciling spirits. He died in Port-au-Prince, Saint-Domingue, in 1774, his Traite sur la reintegration des etres unfinished.
figures.facet: He shows theurgy as empiricism: the Elus Coens ritual is designed to produce verifiable signs, making reconciliation with the divine something operated and witnessed rather than merely believed.
figures.provenance: Traite sur la reintegration des etres (ms., c. 1770s); Elus Coens archives studied by Robert Amadou and Gerard van Rijnberk; Antoine Faivre's scholarship on Christian theosophy.

figures.gloss: Pasqually's secretary (1743-1803), who signed his books 'le Philosophe Inconnu' and gave the tradition its name. He gradually set aside his master's ceremonial theurgy for an inward path, and late in life translated Jacob Boehme into French, fusing the reintegration doctrine with Boehme's theosophy.
figures.facet: He is the rare esotericist who abandoned the machinery of ritual at the height of his initiation, arguing that the only temple that matters is interior — a renunciation that itself founded a lineage.
figures.provenance: Des erreurs et de la verite (1775); L'Homme de desir (1790); Le Ministere de l'homme-esprit (1802); Robert Amadou's critical editions; A.E. Waite's biography.

figures.gloss: The core doctrine, from Pasqually's Traite: humanity was emanated as a divine agent, fell through Adam's 'prevarication' into material bondage, and must be restored — reintegrated — into its 'first estate' of divine powers, and with it all fallen beings. The whole Martinist work, theurgic or interior, is the operation of this return.
figures.facet: It reads the Fall as a cosmic demotion of function rather than a moral stain: man is a displaced officer of creation, and salvation is reinstatement to a post, not pardon for a crime.
figures.provenance: Traite sur la reintegration des etres; expounded across Saint-Martin's works; analyzed by Antoine Faivre and Arthur McCalla in studies of illuminism.

figures.gloss: The Martinist name for Christ: the divine agent who descended into the fallen order to reopen the way of reintegration that Adam closed. In Saint-Martin's later writing the Repairer works through the inner Word, regenerating the 'man of desire' from within.
figures.facet: Christ re-titled by function — not judge or king but Repairer, the being whose whole identity is the reversal of a specific cosmic accident.
figures.provenance: Saint-Martin, L'Homme de desir (1790) and Le Ministere de l'homme-esprit (1802); Pasqually's Traite (the Reconciliateur).

figures.gloss: Saint-Martin's 'inward way': prayer, desire, and interior regeneration through the Word, deliberately preferred over his master's ceremonial operations. The heart, not the ritual chamber, is where the fallen divine faculty of speech-with-God is rekindled.
figures.facet: It internalizes an entire ceremonial system without repudiating it — the theurgic operation re-performed as a movement of desire, keeping the structure and discarding the apparatus.
figures.provenance: Saint-Martin's correspondence with Kirchberger (1792-97) and late works; the 'voie cardiaque' as framed in Martinist historiography (Amadou, Faivre).

figures.gloss: The physician-occultist (1865-1916) who organized the modern Ordre Martiniste (Supreme Council, 1891), claiming an initiatic chain back to Saint-Martin through Chaboseau — a lineage scholars regard as unverifiable. Author of Le Tarot des Bohemiens (1889) and a hub of the Belle Epoque French occult revival; he died in 1916 after serving as a military doctor.
figures.facet: He converted a philosopher's private posterity into an international initiatic order — demonstrating how modern occultism manufactures continuity, chartering the past it needs.
figures.provenance: Papus, Traite elementaire de science occulte (1888) and Le Tarot des Bohemiens (1889); order history in Marie-Sophie Andre & Christophe Beaufils's biography of Papus; David Allen Harvey, 'Beyond Enlightenment' (2005).

figures.gloss: Lyon silk merchant (1730-1824) and Elus Coens initiate under Pasqually who, after the founder's death, built the Rectified Scottish Rite and its Chevaliers Bienfaisants de la Cité Sainte (established at the Convent of the Gauls, 1778, and confirmed at the Congress of Wilhelmsbad, 1782), embedding the reintegration doctrine in a Masonic rite still worked today.
figures.facet: He shows how an esoteric deposit survives its founder's death: rather than transmitting Pasqually's theurgy intact, Willermoz re-housed its doctrine inside durable Masonic institutional form, trading the equinoctial operations for a rite that could outlive any single order.
figures.provenance: Willermoz's Lyon archives and correspondence, studied by René Le Forestier (La Franc-Maçonnerie templière et occultiste) and Antoine Faivre; Rectified Scottish Rite (CBCS) constitutional history from the Convent of the Gauls (1778) and Wilhelmsbad (1782).

figures.gloss: The 'Unknown Superior' degree crowning the grade sequence (Associate, Initiate, S.I.) of the modern Ordre Martiniste Papus organized in 1891, conferring the mask, cloak, and the charge to work anonymously — its name echoing Saint-Martin's own pen-name, the Unknown Philosopher.
figures.facet: The order's defining rite makes anonymity itself the highest attainment: where most initiatory systems crown the adept with a title, Martinism crowns its members with namelessness, modeling the grade on its founder's own chosen erasure of his identity.
figures.provenance: Ordre Martiniste grade structure as documented in Papus's own organizational writings and in Martinist historiography (Robert Amadou, Antoine Faivre); Marie-Sophie André & Christophe Beaufils's biography of Papus.

figures.gloss: French esotericist (1907-1997) who, during the wartime occupation, revived the Ordre des Elus Coens (1943, using the historical name in modernized form) and went on to head the Ordre Martiniste des Elus Coens — the principal 20th-century transmitter through whom most living Martinist and Elus Coens bodies trace their descent.
figures.facet: He re-fused the two poles the tradition's own founders had split apart: Pasqually's ceremonial theurgy and Saint-Martin's interior order, reunited a century and a half later under one 20th-century organizer's hand.
figures.provenance: Ambelain's own historical and ritual writings on the Elus Coens revival; order-lineage history covered in Martinist historiography (Amadou, Faivre) and general surveys of 20th-century French occultism.

edges.note: Intra-tradition: the theurgic master and the secretary who interiorized his system — ceremony and heart as the tradition's two poles.
Intra-tradition: Papus's 1891 order institutionalized Saint-Martin's legacy; the claimed person-to-person initiatic chain between them is contested and unverifiable.
The Elus Coens was chartered as high-grade Freemasonry, recruiting through and building its theurgic grades atop the Masonic craft degrees.
Pasqually's Traite is an esoteric exegesis of Genesis: reintegration is the doctrine of the Fall run in reverse, restoring Adam's estate rather than atoning for his sin.
The Repairer is explicitly Christ, re-read through the reintegration doctrine as the reopener of humanity's lost divine function.

Documented exchange: Papus sat on the supreme council of the Ordre Kabbalistique de la Rose-Croix (founded 1888 with Stanislas de Guaita), and Martinist grades fed its Rosicrucian curriculum — the French occult revival wearing the rose-cross emblem.

Papus's Le Tarot des Bohemiens (1889) and the Golden Dawn's Book of Thoth are twin systematizations of Eliphas Levi's Tarot-Kabbalah key; the Golden Dawn's own Tarot synthesis names Papus among its sources.
Both frame salvation as the return of a fallen divine element to its origin — though Martinism restores a functioning agent of God where gnosis frees a trapped fragment of light.

An Elus Coens initiate under Pasqually, Willermoz carried the reintegration doctrine forward into the Rectified Scottish Rite after the founder's death in Saint-Domingue — attested transmission from theurgist to organizer.
Both were Pasqually's Lyon-circle initiates who took the Elus Coens deposit in opposite directions — Willermoz into an enduring Masonic rite, Saint-Martin into the interior way.

The Rectified Scottish Rite Willermoz built at the Convent of the Gauls (1778) and Wilhelmsbad (1782) is worked atop the Craft degrees, embedding Pasqually's doctrine in mainstream Freemasonry as the Elus Coens itself had been.
The crowning grade of the Ordre Martiniste Papus organized in 1891, conferred after the Associate and Initiate degrees.
The degree's mask and anonymity echo Saint-Martin's own pen-name, the Unknown Philosopher — the order's initiatic namelessness modeled on its founder's own chosen erasure.
The principal 20th-century transmitter following the modern order's founder — Ambelain's wartime reconstruction re-founding the ceremonial pole after Papus's own line had lapsed.
Ambelain's 1943 revival of the Ordre des Elus Coens re-activated Pasqually's 18th-century theurgic deposit after roughly a century and a half dormant.

dossier.essence: Martinism is a current of Christian esotericism born in eighteenth-century France from two masters: Martinez de Pasqually, whose Ordre des Chevaliers Maçons Élus Coëns de l'Univers practiced ceremonial theurgy atop the Masonic degrees from the 1760s, and his secretary Louis-Claude de Saint-Martin, 'the Unknown Philosopher,' who set the ceremonies aside for a purely interior path. It was reorganized as an initiatic order by Papus (Gérard Encausse) in 1891 and survives today in a family of international Martinist orders.
dossier.cosmos: Its core doctrine is the reintegration of beings, from Pasqually's unfinished Traité: humanity was emanated as a divine agent, fell through Adam's 'prevarication' into bondage in matter, and must be restored — with all fallen beings — to its 'first estate' of divine powers. The Fall is read as a demotion of function rather than a moral stain, and Christ appears under the title of the Repairer (le Réparateur), the agent who reopened the way of return. Saint-Martin's later work, steeped in his translations of Jacob Boehme, locates the whole drama within the regeneration of the inner man.
dossier.practice: The tradition holds two poles of practice: Pasqually's ceremonial 'operations' — equinoctial invocations seeking luminous 'passes' as witnessed signs of contact with reconciling spirits — and Saint-Martin's 'way of the heart,' prayer and desire replacing the ritual chamber. Modern Martinist orders in Papus's line transmit lodge-based initiation through a short series of degrees culminating in the Supérieur Inconnu ('Unknown Superior'), joined to meditation and study of the Christian esoteric corpus; the person-to-person chain claimed back to Saint-Martin is regarded by scholars as unverifiable.
dossier.canon: Pasqually's Traité sur la réintégration des êtres (manuscript, c. 1770s; first published 1899) and Saint-Martin's books — Des erreurs et de la vérité (1775), L'Homme de désir (1790), Le Ministère de l'homme-esprit (1802) — with Papus's manuals serving the modern orders.
dossier.reflects: The choice esotericism keeps posing between operation and interiority — a ceremonial system so fully internalized by its own heir that the renunciation of ritual itself became the lineage.
sources: Arthur Edward Waite, 'The Life of Louis Claude de Saint-Martin, the Unknown Philosopher' (1901)
David Allen Harvey, 'Beyond Enlightenment: Occultism and Politics in Modern France' (2005)
Robert Amadou (ed.), critical editions of Saint-Martin and the Élus Coëns corpus

links.cite: Arthur Edward Waite, 'The Life of Louis Claude de Saint-Martin, the Unknown Philosopher' (1901)
David Allen Harvey, 'Beyond Enlightenment: Occultism and Politics in Modern France' (2005)
